name: Wang Ming Dao
source_summary: Wang Ming Dao (1900–1991) was a Chinese independent evangelical pastor who led a house church in Beijing for thirty years. He refused to join the Communist government's Three-Self Patriotic Movement on theological grounds. Arrested in 1955, he initially signed a recantation under sustained psychological pressure, then recanted the recantation and was re-arrested in 1958. He served 23 years in prison before his release in 1980, and continued to encourage the underground church until his death in 1991.
adult_story:
Wang Ming Dao was born in Beijing in 1900, the year of the Boxer Rebellion — an uprising during which his father, a pastor, died before Wang's birth. Raised in poverty by his widowed mother, he came to faith as a young man and in 1925 founded an independent house church in Beijing, which he led for thirty years without denominational affiliation. He believed the church must be subject to Christ and Scripture alone, and he would not compromise that conviction for any earthly authority.

When the People's Republic of China was established in 1949, the Communist government moved to consolidate all Protestant Christianity under the state-controlled Three-Self Patriotic Movement — a church that would be self-governing, self-supporting, and self-propagating under Party oversight. Wang refused to join, not on political grounds but on spiritual ones. He argued that the Three-Self movement had admitted theological liberals and unbelievers into positions of leadership, and that participation would mean fellowship with those who denied the gospel. He preached and wrote against the movement, calling believers to costly faithfulness to the Word of God.

In August 1955, Wang and his wife Deborah were arrested. Under prolonged psychological pressure and physical duress, Wang signed a confession in 1956 agreeing that he had been a counter-revolutionary reactionary. He was released — but could not live with what he had done. In his own words, he had been a Peter who denied his Lord. In 1958 he recanted his confession and was re-arrested. He would not be released until 1980, having spent twenty-three years in prison.

Wang emerged elderly, nearly blind, and physically frail — yet unbroken in faith. He continued to meet with believers, speak, and encourage the underground church until his death in 1991. His life became a pattern for Chinese Christians of costly, enduring faithfulness in the face of state opposition.

His story is not one of seamless heroism but of grace — the kind that reaches a man who has fallen, restores him, and holds him to the end. He fell, as Peter fell. And like Peter, he was restored — not by his own strength of will, but by the mercy of a Lord who does not abandon those who stumble back to Him. His decades of suffering bore witness to a truth that no prison can contain: that the gospel of Jesus Christ is worth more than liberty, comfort, or life itself.
family_story:
Wang Ming Dao grew up in Beijing, China, a long time ago. His family was very poor, and his father had died before he was even born. But as a young man, Wang discovered something more precious than wealth — he came to trust in Jesus Christ.

Wang started a church in Beijing, teaching the Bible to whoever would come. But then the government tried to take control of all the churches in China. Wang believed this was wrong — the church belongs to Jesus, not to any government. So he refused to join the government's church.

Because of this, Wang was arrested and put in prison. He stayed there for twenty-three years. That is a very long time!

Wang made a mistake during those painful years — for a short while, under great pressure, he said what the government wanted him to say, even though it was not true. But then he took it back. He said, 'I have denied my Lord. I must confess it.' And he went back to prison rather than live a lie.

When Wang finally came out of prison, he was old and nearly blind — but his love for Jesus was still strong. He kept meeting with other Christians and encouraging them until the end of his life.

Wang's story shows us that following Jesus can cost us greatly. But it also shows us something wonderful: that Jesus never abandons those who trust in Him, even when they stumble. That is what grace means — and Wang's whole life is full of it.

name: John Newton
hymns: Glorious Things of Thee Are Spoken
How Sweet the Name of Jesus Sounds
source_summary: John Newton (1725–1807) was an English Anglican minister and hymn writer. A former slave trader, he experienced a spiritual awakening during a violent storm at sea in 1748 and was gradually converted to evangelical Christianity. He was ordained in 1764, served the parish of Olney in Buckinghamshire, and co-produced the Olney Hymns (1779) with poet William Cowper, including Amazing Grace. In his later years he became an outspoken opponent of the slave trade and gave evidence to Parliament during William Wilberforce's abolitionist campaign.
adult_story:
John Newton was born in London in 1725, the son of a merchant sea captain. His mother, a devout Nonconformist Christian, died when he was six, and whatever faith she had passed to him faded through a turbulent childhood and young adulthood at sea. By his mid-twenties Newton had sunk to what he himself later called the lowest moral depths — working in the slave trade, indifferent to the suffering of those he transported, and openly contemptuous of religion.

The event that first cracked open his conscience came during a violent storm off the coast of Newfoundland in March 1748. Convinced the ship would sink, Newton cried out to God in what he later described as an instinctive, desperate prayer. The storm passed. The ship survived. He would write later that he could not claim a full conversion in that moment — but it was the beginning of a long awakening. He continued in the slave trade for several more years, until a serious illness in 1754 brought his seafaring to a close.

Newton was ordained as an Anglican minister in 1764, assigned to the parish of Olney in Buckinghamshire. There, in partnership with the poet William Cowper, he produced the Olney Hymns in 1779 — a collection of 348 hymns including Amazing Grace, perhaps the most widely sung English hymn in history. The hymn is rooted entirely in Newton's own experience of being a wretch reclaimed by undeserved mercy. It expresses the doctrine of sovereign grace with uncommon plainness: a great God seeking and saving a sinner who could not save himself.

Newton became a committed opponent of the slave trade in his later years. In 1788 he published Thoughts Upon the African Slave Trade, a pamphlet recounting with evident shame the horrors he had witnessed and participated in. He gave evidence before Parliament during Wilberforce's campaign for abolition. He died in 1807 — the very year the slave trade was abolished in the British Empire — having said in his final years: 'My memory is nearly gone, but I remember two things: that I am a great sinner, and that Christ is a great Saviour.'

That sentence is the whole of Newton's gospel — not the redemption of a good man who made mistakes, but the grace of God poured out on one who knew himself to be entirely unworthy. Amazing Grace is theology in verse: the astonishment of a man who looked at his own life and could only marvel that God had found him at all.
family_story:
Long ago in England, there lived a young man named John Newton who made terrible choices. He sailed the seas and was part of a business that was cruel and wrong — helping to take people from Africa and sell them as slaves. John knew deep down it was wrong, but he kept doing it anyway.

One night, John was on a ship in a terrible storm. The waves were enormous and he thought the ship was going to sink. Frightened and desperate, he called out to God for help — perhaps for the first time in years. The storm passed, and John was safe. Something had changed inside him.

Over time, John Newton changed completely. He became a pastor and spent his life telling people about Jesus and how much God loves sinners. He also helped fight to end the slave trade he had once been part of — and he was brave enough to tell Parliament the truth about how wrong it was.

John wrote a hymn that people all around the world still sing today — Amazing Grace. When he wrote the words 'a wretch like me,' he really meant them. He knew exactly what kind of person he had been. But he also knew that Jesus had saved him anyway.

The wonderful news of Jesus is that He saves people just like John — people who have done wrong things, people who feel far from God, people who do not deserve to be saved. That is what grace means. John Newton spent the rest of his long life amazed by it, and his hymn still helps us feel that same amazement.

name: Fanny Crosby
hymns: Safe in the Arms of Jesus
source_summary: Fanny Crosby (1820–1915) was an American hymn writer and rescue mission worker. Blinded in infancy due to a medical error, she came to an evangelical conversion experience in 1850 and subsequently wrote an estimated 8,000–9,000 hymn texts, including Blessed Assurance, To God Be the Glory, and Safe in the Arms of Jesus. She was deeply involved in rescue mission work among the poor in New York City and died at age 94 in Bridgeport, Connecticut.
adult_story:
Fanny Crosby was born on 24 March 1820 in Putnam County, New York. At six weeks of age, an eye inflammation was treated with warm mustard poultices by a man filling in for the family doctor — a treatment that permanently destroyed her sight. She would be blind for the rest of her ninety-four years.

What is remarkable about Crosby's story is not that she triumphed over blindness, but that she consistently refused to treat it as a tragedy requiring triumph. In her autobiography she wrote: 'It seemed intended by the blessed providence of God that I should be blind all my life, and I thank Him for the dispensation. If perfect earthly sight were offered me tomorrow I would not accept it. I might not have sung hymns to the praise of God if I had been distracted by the beautiful and interesting things about me.' This was not naïve contentment — it was a settled theological conviction that God orders all things for the good of those who love Him.

Crosby came to an evangelical conversion experience in 1850 at a Methodist church in New York City. From that point, her hymn-writing became the vehicle for an astonishing outpouring. Working with composers including William Bradbury, Phoebe Knapp, and Ira Sankey, she produced somewhere between 8,000 and 9,000 hymn texts — often writing several per day by dictating to a scribe, since she kept all her verse in her memory. Such was the volume of her output that publishers sometimes issued her hymns under pseudonyms to avoid any single author dominating a collection.

Among her best-known works are Blessed Assurance, To God Be the Glory, Safe in the Arms of Jesus, Near the Cross, and Rescue the Perishing. These hymns share a consistent theological weight: the assurance of salvation in Christ, the sufficiency of His atoning work, and the certainty of God's love for the sinner. Blessed Assurance — with its refrain, 'This is my story, this is my song, praising my Saviour all the day long' — distils the essence of evangelical experience into verse of memorable plainness.

Crosby spent much of her adult life in New York City, deeply committed to rescue mission work among the poor, the homeless, and those struggling with alcoholism. She died in 1915 in Bridgeport, Connecticut, having outlived most of her contemporaries and produced a body of hymnody still in active use in churches around the world.

Her life is a rebuke to self-pity and a witness to the truth that God uses limited, broken vessels for extraordinary purposes. The blindness that might have silenced her instead directed her gaze inward and upward, producing a river of praise that has carried millions toward the mercy of Christ.
family_story:
When Fanny Crosby was a tiny baby, a doctor made a mistake while treating an eye infection — and Fanny became blind. She could not see anything at all for her entire long life. She lived to be ninety-four years old!

You might think that would make her very sad. But Fanny said she was actually thankful. She believed that God had a special plan for her, and that not being able to see helped her focus on what really mattered — Jesus.

When Fanny was about thirty years old, she gave her heart to Jesus at a church meeting. After that, she started writing hymns — songs to sing to God and about God. She wrote so many of them. About eight thousand! Because she could not see, she kept every song in her head and spoke them out loud for someone else to write down.

Some of Fanny's songs are still sung in churches all over the world today: Blessed Assurance, To God Be the Glory, and Safe in the Arms of Jesus. These songs help people remember that Jesus loves them and that they can trust Him completely.

Fanny's life shows us something wonderful: God can use anyone — even someone the world might feel sorry for. Fanny did not feel sorry for herself. She spent her whole life praising the Saviour who loved her. And her songs still help people do exactly the same thing, even today.

name: John Bunyan
source_summary: John Bunyan (1628–1688) was an English Nonconformist preacher and author born in Elstow, Bedfordshire, to a tinker's family. He came to faith through prolonged spiritual struggle and became a lay preacher with the Bedford Nonconformist congregation. He was imprisoned twice for preaching without a licence, the first imprisonment lasting twelve years. During a second, shorter imprisonment (1676–77) he wrote The Pilgrim's Progress (1678), the second most widely translated book in history after the Bible. His spiritual autobiography Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners was published in 1666.
adult_story:
John Bunyan was born in 1628 in Elstow, Bedfordshire, the son of a tinker — a mender of pots and pans. His education was minimal and his early life unremarkable. By his own account in Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners, his spiritual state as a young man was alarming: he was tormented by a conscience awakening to the holiness of God, and what might seem like modest offences became, in that light, symptoms of a heart entirely at war with its Maker.

His conversion was prolonged and anguished. For years he was assailed by blasphemous thoughts he feared were evidence of his own reprobation, and haunted by a biblical text he could not shake — the story of Esau, who for a bowl of soup sold his birthright and found no place of repentance, though he sought it with tears. Bunyan feared he had done the same: sold his soul and found no way back. The eventual resolution — the settled conviction that Christ's blood was more than sufficient, that the promises of God were more solid than his own fears — transformed not only his inner life but his preaching. He joined the Bedford Nonconformist congregation and became a lay preacher of evident power.

In 1660, following the Restoration of Charles II and the reimposition of the Church of England's monopoly on preaching, Bunyan was arrested for preaching without a licence. He refused to stop, and spent twelve years in Bedford Gaol — with a brief period of release in 1666, after which he was imprisoned again. A second, shorter imprisonment in 1676–77 gave him the conditions in which he wrote The Pilgrim's Progress.

Published in 1678, The Pilgrim's Progress is an allegory of the Christian life, following a man named Christian from the City of Destruction to the Celestial City. It is the second most widely translated book in history after the Bible, and has never been out of print. Through characters such as Evangelist, Faithful, Hopeful, and Mr Worldly Wiseman, Bunyan maps the inner landscape of the soul under grace — its temptations, fears, doubts, and ultimate assurance. The theology is unambiguous: the burden of sin falls from Christian's shoulders not through human effort or religious rite, but at the foot of the cross, where he sees the crucified and risen Christ.

Bunyan died in 1688, having served his congregation faithfully through poverty and persecution. His works are a monument to what God can do through a man with no formal education, no wealth, no social standing — equipped with nothing but a Bible, a burdened conscience, and the astonishing relief of discovering that Christ had borne his sin.
family_story:
John Bunyan grew up in England a very long time ago. His family was poor — his father mended pots and pans for a living. John did not get much schooling, and as a young man he felt the weight of his sins pressing on him. He knew something was wrong inside, but he did not know how to fix it.

After many difficult years of searching, John came to understand that Jesus had died for his sins — and that by trusting in Jesus, all that weight could be forgiven and taken away. He became a preacher, telling everyone this wonderful news.

But the king's government said only official church ministers were allowed to preach. John refused to stop preaching about Jesus. So he was put in prison. He spent twelve years there!

While in prison, John had a lot of time to think and write. He wrote a book called The Pilgrim's Progress — a story about a man named Christian who travels from a dangerous city toward a beautiful heavenly city. On the way, Christian faces scary valleys, wicked giants, and many temptations. But he keeps going, because Jesus has promised him a home at the end of the road.

People have been reading The Pilgrim's Progress for over three hundred years. It helps us understand what following Jesus is really like — not always easy, but always worth it.

John's life shows us that God uses ordinary people in extraordinary ways. John was just a poor tinker's son who trusted in Christ. And that was more than enough.
